Values:
BEJSON Crash Course

Document Version 23 · MFDB Spec 1.31 · Formats: 104 · 104a · 104db Format Creator: Elton Boehnen · https:/boehnenelton2024.pages.dev

BEJSON

BEJSON is a strict, self-describing tabular format built on JSON. Its defining rule is positional integrity:

  • "Fields[n]" always maps to "Values[][n]"
  • Record lengths must exactly equal "Fields.length"
  • "null" preserves structure for absent data

This enables:

  • Fast index-based access
  • Strong typing
  • Embedded schema validation
  • No external schema files

Core Structure (All Versions)

{ "Format": "BEJSON", "Format_Version": "104" | "104a" | "104db", "Format_Creator": "Elton Boehnen", "Records_Type": [], "Fields": [], "Values": [[]] }

Field Definition Schema

{ "name": "string", "type": "string|integer|number|boolean|array|object", "Record_Type_Parent": "string (104db only)" }

Universal Rules

  • All 6 mandatory top-level keys are required
  • "Format_Creator" must equal ""Elton Boehnen""
  • Duplicate field names are invalid
  • "null" is valid for any type
  • Every record length must equal "Fields.length"
  • Append new fields only to the end of "Fields"

BEJSON 104

Single-entity format with full JSON type support.

Rules

  • "Records_Type" must contain exactly 1 string
  • No custom top-level headers
  • Exception: optional "Parent_Hierarchy"
  • Supports "array" and "object"

Best Use Cases

  • Telemetry
  • AI memory snapshots
  • Logs
  • Archives
  • Scientific datasets

Schema Example

{ "Format": "BEJSON", "Format_Version": "104", "Format_Creator": "Elton Boehnen", "Records_Type": ["DroneFlight"], "Fields": [ {"name":"drone_id","type":"string"}, {"name":"pilot","type":"string"}, {"name":"altitude_m","type":"number"}, {"name":"battery_cells","type":"array"}, {"name":"weather","type":"object"} ], "Values": [] }

Example Data

{ "Format": "BEJSON", "Format_Version": "104", "Format_Creator": "Elton Boehnen", "Records_Type": ["DroneFlight"], "Fields": [ {"name":"drone_id","type":"string"}, {"name":"pilot","type":"string"}, {"name":"altitude_m","type":"number"}, {"name":"battery_cells","type":"array"}, {"name":"weather","type":"object"} ], "Values": [ [ "DR-77", "Mira", 412.5, [4.19,4.18,4.17,4.20], {"wind_kmh":18,"condition":"Cloudy"} ], [ "DR-91", "Jules", 188.2, null, {"wind_kmh":5,"condition":"Clear"} ] ] }

BEJSON 104a

Single-entity format optimized for lightweight metadata and configuration.

Rules

  • "Records_Type" must contain exactly 1 string
  • Primitive types only:
  • "string"
  • "integer"
  • "number"
  • "boolean"
  • Custom top-level headers allowed
  • Custom headers:
  • Must use PascalCase
  • Cannot conflict with mandatory keys
  • File-level metadata only

Best Use Cases

  • Configs
  • Runtime policies
  • Health checks
  • Registries
  • Feature flags

Schema Example

{ "Format": "BEJSON", "Format_Version": "104a", "Format_Creator": "Elton Boehnen", "DeploymentZone": "Europa-West", "Records_Type": ["FeatureToggle"], "Fields": [ {"name":"feature_name","type":"string"}, {"name":"enabled","type":"boolean"}, {"name":"rollout_percent","type":"integer"} ], "Values": [] }

Example Data

{ "Format": "BEJSON", "Format_Version": "104a", "Format_Creator": "Elton Boehnen", "DeploymentZone": "Europa-West", "Environment": "Production", "Records_Type": ["FeatureToggle"], "Fields": [ {"name":"feature_name","type":"string"}, {"name":"enabled","type":"boolean"}, {"name":"rollout_percent","type":"integer"} ], "Values": [ ["voice_assistant_v2",true,100], ["experimental_ui",false,0], ["smart_cache",true,35] ] }

BEJSON 104db

Single-file multi-entity relational format.

Rules

  • "Records_Type" must contain 2+ unique entity names
  • No custom top-level headers
  • First field must be:

{"name":"Record_Type_Parent","type":"string"}

  • Every non-discriminator field must declare:
  • ""Record_Type_Parent":"EntityName""
  • Position 0 of every record must match a valid entity name
  • Non-applicable entity fields must be "null"
  • Supports "array" and "object"

Relationship Convention

Foreign keys should use "_fk" suffix:

captain_id_fk

Convention only — not enforced.

Best Use Cases

  • Portable relational datasets
  • AI-readable databases
  • Small game worlds
  • Embedded tooling state

Limitations

Because all entities share one table, null-padding grows rapidly as fields and records increase.

Recommended for:

  • Small/medium relational datasets
  • AI-editable databases
  • Portable single-file systems

Schema Example

{ "Format":"BEJSON", "Format_Version":"104db", "Format_Creator":"Elton Boehnen", "Records_Type":["Spaceship","Mission"], "Fields":[ {"name":"Record_Type_Parent","type":"string"}, {"name":"ship_id","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Spaceship"}, {"name":"captain","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Spaceship"}, {"name":"mission_id","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Mission"}, {"name":"ship_id_fk","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Mission"} ], "Values":[] }

Example Data

{ "Format":"BEJSON", "Format_Version":"104db", "Format_Creator":"Elton Boehnen", "Records_Type":["Spaceship","Mission"], "Fields":[ {"name":"Record_Type_Parent","type":"string"}, {"name":"ship_id","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Spaceship"}, {"name":"captain","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Spaceship"}, {"name":"mission_id","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Mission"}, {"name":"ship_id_fk","type":"string","Record_Type_Parent":"Mission"} ], "Values":[ ["Spaceship","NX-01","Captain Vale",null,null], ["Spaceship","CR-9","Commander Ilya",null,null], ["Mission",null,null,"MS-44","NX-01"], ["Mission",null,null,"MS-77","CR-9"] ] }

BEJSON Validation Summary

A BEJSON file is valid when:

  • JSON syntax is valid
  • Mandatory keys exist
  • Field names are unique
  • Record lengths match "Fields"
  • Values match declared types
  • Version-specific rules are satisfied

Version| Rules 104| Single entity, complex types allowed, no custom headers except "Parent_Hierarchy" 104a| Single entity, primitive-only, custom metadata allowed 104db| Multi-entity, mandatory "Record_Type_Parent", null-padding required

BEJSON Best Practices

  • Use "snake_case" field names
  • Use PascalCase custom headers in 104a
  • Use "_fk" for foreign keys
  • Use "null" for absent values
  • Treat field reordering/removal as breaking schema changes
  • Split large datasets across files/databases
  • Encrypt sensitive data

BEJSON Capability Matrix

Feature| 104| 104a| 104db Record Types| Single| Single| Multiple Complex Types| Yes| No| Yes Custom Headers| No*| Yes| No Multi-Entity| No| No| Yes FK Convention| Optional| Optional| Recommended

  • "Parent_Hierarchy" is allowed in 104.

MFDB (Multifile Database)

MFDB is a database architecture built on top of BEJSON.

It is not a new BEJSON version.

MFDB combines:

  • One BEJSON 104a manifest
  • Multiple BEJSON 104 entity files

Every file remains valid standalone BEJSON.

MFDB Structure

vault_db/ ├── 104a.mfdb.bejson └── data/ ├── explorer.bejson └── artifact.bejson

Rules:

  • Manifest filename is fixed:
  • "104a.mfdb.bejson"
  • Entity files default to:
  • "data/.bejson"
  • Paths must remain relative
  • Paths cannot escape database root

MFDB Manifest

The manifest is always BEJSON 104a.

Required Headers

Header| Type MFDB_Version| string DB_Name| string

Optional Headers

Header| Type DB_Description| string Schema_Version| string Author| string Created_At| string Network_Role| string

"Network_Role" values:

  • ""Master""
  • ""Slave""

Required Fields

Field| Type entity_name| string file_path| string

Optional Standard Fields

Field| Type description| string record_count| integer schema_version| string primary_key| string

MFDB Manifest Schema Example

{ "Format":"BEJSON", "Format_Version":"104a", "Format_Creator":"Elton Boehnen", "MFDB_Version":"1.31", "DB_Name":"AncientVault", "Records_Type":["mfdb"], "Fields":[ {"name":"entity_name","type":"string"}, {"name":"file_path","type":"string"}, {"name":"primary_key","type":"string"} ], "Values":[] }

MFDB Manifest Example

{ "Format":"BEJSON", "Format_Version":"104a", "Format_Creator":"Elton Boehnen", "MFDB_Version":"1.31", "DB_Name":"AncientVault", "Schema_Version":"2.4.0", "Records_Type":["mfdb"], "Fields":[ {"name":"entity_name","type":"string"}, {"name":"file_path","type":"string"}, {"name":"primary_key","type":"string"} ], "Values":[ ["Explorer","data/explorer.bejson","explorer_id"], ["Artifact","data/artifact.bejson","artifact_id"] ] }

MFDB Entity Rules

Entity files are BEJSON 104 documents.

Required:

  • "Format_Version == "104""
  • "Records_Type" contains exactly one entity name
  • Entity name must match manifest registration
  • "Parent_Hierarchy" must exist
  • "Parent_Hierarchy" must resolve back to manifest

Example:

"Parent_Hierarchy":"../104a.mfdb.bejson"

Unlike 104db:

  • MFDB stores one entity per file
  • No structural null-padding
  • Records remain dense

MFDB Entity Example: Explorer

{ "Format":"BEJSON", "Format_Version":"104", "Format_Creator":"Elton Boehnen", "Parent_Hierarchy":"../104a.mfdb.bejson", "Records_Type":["Explorer"], "Fields":[ {"name":"explorer_id","type":"string"}, {"name":"callsign","type":"string"}, {"name":"sector","type":"string"} ], "Values":[ ["E-01","GhostFox","Outer Rim"], ["E-02","NovaEcho","Crystal Basin"] ] }

MFDB Entity Example: Artifact

{ "Format":"BEJSON", "Format_Version":"104", "Format_Creator":"Elton Boehnen", "Parent_Hierarchy":"../104a.mfdb.bejson", "Records_Type":["Artifact"], "Fields":[ {"name":"artifact_id","type":"string"}, {"name":"owner_explorer_id_fk","type":"string"}, {"name":"artifact_name","type":"string"} ], "Values":[ ["A-44","E-01","Solar Compass"], ["A-91","E-02","Echo Prism"] ] }

MFDB Discovery Rules

A tool can identify MFDB files locally:

Condition| Result "104a" + filename ends ".mfdb.bejson"| Manifest "104" + "Parent_Hierarchy" present| Entity Otherwise| Standalone BEJSON

No directory scanning required.

MFDB Relationships

MFDB does not enforce joins or referential integrity.

Relationships are inferred through:

  • "_fk" suffixes
  • "primary_key" declarations in manifest

Example:

owner_explorer_id_fk → explorer_id

Unresolved FKs should produce warnings, not hard failures.

MFDB Validation Levels

Level 1 — Manifest

Checks:

  • Valid BEJSON 104a
  • "Records_Type == ["mfdb"]"
  • Unique "entity_name"
  • Unique "file_path"
  • All paths exist

Level 2 — Entity

Checks:

  • Valid BEJSON 104
  • "Parent_Hierarchy" exists
  • Parent manifest resolves
  • Entity matches manifest registration

Level 3 — Database

Checks:

  • All entities pass validation
  • Optional FK resolution
  • Optional "record_count" verification

Warnings do not invalidate the database.

Validation vs Standardization

Validation

Structural correctness.

Failure result:

  • Hard error
  • Database considered broken

Standardization

Architectural compliance.

Examples:

  • snake_case fields
  • PascalCase entities
  • "_id" primary keys
  • "_fk" foreign keys
  • Federation headers

Failure result:

  • Non-standard warning only

Federation (MFDB 1.31)

MFDB 1.31 introduces lightweight distributed roles.

Roles

Role| Purpose Master| Authoritative node Slave| Replicated operational node

Design Goals

  • Zero-bloat AI context
  • Structural blindness
  • Atomic updates
  • Portable replication

Communication Model

One-Way Push

Slave distills and pushes logs upward.

Inverse Drop-Zone Polling

Master atomically replaces policy/config files inside Slave directories using filesystem rename operations.

MFDB Best Practices

  • Keep one manifest per database
  • Keep manifest at database root
  • Use atomic writes ("temp → rename")
  • Validate bidirectionally
  • Use semantic schema versions
  • Append fields only
  • Encrypt sensitive data
  • Split very large datasets into multiple MFDB databases

What MFDB Is Not

MFDB is not:

  • A new BEJSON version
  • A query language
  • A runtime database engine
  • A replacement for SQL
  • A replacement for 104db

Use:

  • "104db" for small single-file relational datasets
  • "MFDB" for scalable multi-file entity separation

Final Summary

Feature| BEJSON 104| BEJSON 104a| BEJSON 104db| MFDB Single File| Yes| Yes| Yes| No Multi-Entity| No| No| Yes| Yes Complex Types| Yes| No| Yes| Yes Custom Headers| No*| Yes| No| Manifest Only Null Padding| No| No| Required| No FK Convention| Optional| Optional| Recommended| Recommended Primary Use| Logs/Data| Configs| Small Relational DB| Multi-File DB

  • Except "Parent_Hierarchy"
